symbols: women surrounding Anthony — the temptation of lust and worldly pleasure
dramatic landscape — the wilderness where spiritual warfare intensifies
fire or explosion in background — demonic destruction and hellfire
distant buildings — the monastery or civilization Anthony left behind
Anthony resisting — the victory of faith over temptation
description: A landscape scene with several figures in the foreground. At center, a dark-robed elderly figure (Anthony) appears to be accosted or surrounded by three or four well-dressed women in red and dark garments who reach toward him. The setting is a rocky, hilly landscape with buildings or a town visible in the middle distance. In the far background, what appears to be fire or an explosion lights up the sky. The palette is dark and moody with greens, browns, and touches of red.
theological_description: Patinir depicts the Temptation of Saint Anthony, the Egyptian desert father (c. 251-356) whose legendary struggles against demonic temptation became a major subject of Christian art. Here the temptations take the form of seductive women — the demon of lust that Anthony had to combat through prayer and fasting. While Anthony is not biblical, his story is rooted in biblical spiritual warfare. Jesus himself was tempted in the wilderness (Matt 4:1-11), establishing the pattern for all Christian asceticism. James counsels: 'Resist the devil, and he will flee from you' (Jas 4:7). Paul warned about the 'cosmic powers over this present darkness' and instructed believers to 'put on the whole armor of God' (Eph 6:11-12). The fire in the background represents the hellish origin of the temptations. Anthony's life of prayer and resistance became the foundation of Christian monasticism and demonstrated that 'greater is he who is in you than he who is in the world' (1 John 4:4).

scripture_refs.reason: Jesus tempted in the wilderness — the pattern for Anthony's desert temptations
Resist the devil and he will flee — Anthony's resistance to temptation
Put on the whole armor of God against cosmic powers of darkness
Greater is he who is in you than he who is in the world
